# An Introduction to Probability Theory

The purpose of statistics is to make sense of the world by analysing data. Typically, however, it is only possible to obtain data from a random sample of the full population of interest, introducing uncertainty that must be accounted for. For this reason, developing an understanding of statistical analysis begins with learning about probability theory.

People talk about *chance* regularly. For example, 'St Mirren are unlikely to win this weekend', or 'It is probably going to rain today'. However, neither of these comments seek to assign a numerical value to quantify those chances. In this chapter, probability as a *numerical measure* will be studied.

#### Key definitions {-}

In probability theory, an experiment for which all possible *outcomes* can be defined, and yet the actual outcome of any given *trial* cannot be known in advance, is called a *random experiment*. The set of all possible outcomes is called the *sample space* of the random experiment. Probability is assigned to individual outcomes or to sets of outcomes, called *events* (subsets of the sample space). Probability can be interpreted as the **long-run relative frequency** of an outcome or event occurring, and values range from 0 (*impossible*) to 1 (*certain*).

\begin{wrapfigure}{r}{0.15\textwidth}
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.15\textwidth]{dice}
\end{wrapfigure}

For example, in the context of rolling a fair, cubical die:

- the procedure of rolling the die and observing the result is a **random experiment**.

- each roll of the die may be referred to as a **trial**.

- the **sample space** consists of a list of all of the possible **outcomes**: $\{\text{1},\text{2},\text{3},\text{4},\text{5},\text{6}\}$

- the **probability** of a $5$ is $\frac{\text{1}}{\text{6}}$ since *in the long-run* it can be expected to occur $\frac{\text{1}}{\text{6}}$ of the time.

- a possible **event** of interest may be that the outcome is *prime*: $\{\text{2},\text{3},\text{5}\}$ 

#### Probability notation {-}

The probability that a fair cubical die lands on 5 is *one-in-six*, or $\frac{\text{1}}{\text{6}}$. Using probability notation:

$$\text{P}(\text{rolling a }\text{5})=\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{6}}$$

\pagebreak

## Measuring Probability

#### Experimental Probability {-}

The probability of an event can be estimated through repeated trials - this is referred to as the \mbox{\textit{experimental probability}} of the event. For example, if a motorist passes a set of traffic lights every morning and wishes to know the probability of having to wait at a red light on any given morning, they could record the *proportion* of mornings on which they had to wait at a red light over a period of time. If, over the course of 200 mornings, they have to wait at a red light 114 times:

\vspace{-0.1cm}

$$\text{P}(\text{red light})\approx\frac{\text{114}}{\text{200}}=\text{0.57}$$

\vspace{-0.2cm}

#### Theoretical Probability {-}

When each of the outcomes in the sample space of a random experiment can be reasonably assumed to be \mbox{\textbf{equally likely}}, then the probability of outcomes or events can be determined. This is called *theoretical probability*.

Equally likely outcomes typically arise from the symmetry of a physical object (such as a coin or die) or a reasonable assumption. The probability of any *individual outcome* in a sample space containing $N$ equally likely outcomes is $\frac{\text{1}}{N}$. For example, if a playing card is picked at random from a standard pack of 52 cards, containing no jokers, then the probability of picking the Queen of Hearts is $\frac{\text{1}}{\text{52}}$.

An **event** is a set of one or more outcomes from the sample space. The probability of an event is given by:

\begin{tcolorbox}[center,title=Probability of an Event,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black,width=13cm,halign = center,halign title = center]
\vspace{-0.3cm}
$$\text{P}(\text{event})=\frac{\text{number of outcomes from the sample space in the event}}{\text{total number of outcomes in the sample space}}$$
\end{tcolorbox}


\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!,colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} A bag contains 13 red counters, 5 blue counters and 4 green counters. A counter is chosen at random. Find the probability that the counter is green.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:}
\vspace{-0.3cm}
$$\text{P}(\text{green})=\frac{\text{4}}{\text{22}}=\frac{\text{2}}{\text{11}}$$
\end{tcolorbox}

When considering two events in probability theory, *"and"* means that *both* events are true, and *"or"* means that *at least one* of the events is true (including the possibility that they both occur, where applicable).

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!,colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} A car dealership has 80 cars in its showroom. They have a mix of electric, hybrid, petrol and diesel cars, both new and second-hand. The table below shows the numbers of each type.

\begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{| c|c c c c|} 
      \hline
          & electric & hybrid & petrol & diesel\\ 
      \hline
         new & 15 & 17 & 28 & 3\\ 
         second-hand & 1 & 7 & 4 & 5\\ 
     \hline
    \end{tabular}
\end{center}

A car is selected at random. Determine the probability that the car selected is a second-hand hybrid car.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:}
\vspace{-0.3cm}
$$\text{P}(\text{second-hand and hybrid})=\dfrac{\text{7}}{\text{80}}=\text{0.0875}$$
\end{tcolorbox}

In this textbook, probabilities will be given as fractions or *rounded to four decimal places* for consistency.

\pagebreak

[]{#ex21q}

#### [Exercise 2.1](#ex21a) {-}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item A letter of the English alphabet is chosen at random. State the probability that the letter chosen is:
    \begin{multicols}{3}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item A vowel.
        \item A consonant.
        \item In the word \textit{`statistics'}.
    \end{enumerate}
    \end{multicols}
    \item A bag contains four blue, five green and six red counters. Find the probability that a randomly chosen counter is:
    \begin{multicols}{3}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Blue.
        \item Either green or red.
        \item Not red.
    \end{enumerate}
    \end{multicols}
    \item A box of chocolates contains chocolates of three different flavours. Five are caramel, four are orange and three are strawberry. A chocolate is chosen at random. State the value of:
    \begin{multicols}{3}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item \text{P}(orange).
        \item \text{P}(orange and strawberry).
        \item \text{P}(not caramel)
    \end{enumerate}
    \end{multicols}
    \item The UK contains 76 cities, of which 55 are in England, 8 in Scotland, 7 in Wales and 6 in Northern Ireland. If a city in the UK is selected at random, find the probability that the chosen city is:
    \begin{multicols}{3}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item In Scotland.
        \item In Wales or Northern Ireland.
        \item Not in England.
    \end{enumerate}
    \end{multicols}
    \item A board game uses green counters numbered from 1 to 8, blue counters numbered from 1 to 6 and red counters numbered from 1 to 4. A counter is chosen at random. Determine the probability that the counter selected is:
    \begin{multicols}{3}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item The blue numbered 5.
        \item Numbered 5.
        \item Blue or numbered 5.
    \end{enumerate}
    \end{multicols}
    \item A restaurant grades the fruit contained in a delivery as good or bad. The contents of the previous delivery are shown in the table below.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{| c|c c c|} 
      \hline
          & Apples & Tangerines & Kiwi\\ 
      \hline
         Good & 27 & 19 & 26 \\ 
         Bad & 3 & 1 & 4 \\ 
     \hline
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    Letting $G$ represent that the event the fruit chosen is \textit{good}, and $A$ the event that it is an \textit{apple}, find:
    \begin{multicols}{3}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $\text{P}(G)$
        \item $\text{P}(G\text{ and }A)$
        \item $\text{P}(\text{Not }A)$
    \end{enumerate}
    \end{multicols}
    \item A hospital contains a number of doctors, nurses, allied health professionals and support staff, who are each assigned to work on one of Wards 1 to 3. The breakdown of this is shown in the table below.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{| c|c c c c|} 
      \hline  
          & Doctor & Nurse & Allied & Support\\ 
      \hline
         Ward 1 & 3 & 7 & 5 & 8\\ 
         Ward 2 & 4 & 10 & 2 & 9\\
         Ward 3 & 2 & 8 & 1 & 5\\
     \hline
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    If member of staff working at the hospital is chosen at random, find the probability that they are:
    \begin{multicols}{3}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item A nurse.
        \item Not on Ward 2.
        \item On Ward 2 but not a doctor
    \end{enumerate}
    \end{multicols}
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

## Sample Spaces as Lists and Grids

It can help to write out sample spaces. Some sample spaces can be easily described using a simple list, but more organisation may be needed for more complicated sample spaces, such as approaching the list in a structured manner or using a grid. Sample spaces are particularly useful when each outcome is equally likely, as this allows probabilities to be calculated directly by counting outcomes.. 

If two coins are tossed, it may be tempting to suggest that the probability of both landing tails is one-in-three, since the experiment can result in either no coins landing tails, only one coin landing tails or two coins landing tails. However a list of the four possible *equally likely* outcomes in the sample space shows that this is not the case:

*Not equally likely outcomes:* $\{\text{no tails},\text{one tail},\textbf{two tails}\}$

These three outcomes are *not* equally likely, so the probability is *not one-in-three*, or: $\text{P}(\text{both tails})\ne\frac{\text{1}}{\text{3}}$

*Equally likely outcomes:* $\{\text{HH},\text{HT},\text{TH},\textbf{TT}\}$

These four outcomes *are* equally likely, so the probability is *one-in-four*, or: $\text{P}(\text{both tails})=\frac{\text{1}}{\text{4}}$.

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!,colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} Three coins are tossed at the same time. Find the probability that exactly two of the coins land showing tails.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution: }\textit{A list of equally likely outcomes will help here. Note there are $\text{2}\times \text{2}\times \text{2}=\text{8}$ outcomes.}

$$HHH \quad HHT \quad HTH \quad \underbrace{HTT} \quad THH \quad \underbrace{THT} \quad \underbrace{TTH} \quad TTT$$

$$\text{P}(\text{exactly two tails})=\dfrac{\text{3}}{\text{8}}$$

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!,colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} Two fair dice are rolled. Determine the probability that the sum obtained is at least nine.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution: }\textit{A grid of outcomes will help here. Note there are $\text{6}\times \text{6}=\text{36}$ outcomes.}\vspace{0.5cm}

\begin{tikzpicture}
\fill [gray!40] (-3,-3) rectangle (-2,4);
\fill [gray!40] (-2,3) rectangle (4,4);
\draw[step=1cm,color=gray] (-3,-3) grid (4,4);
\node at (0.5,4.5) {\textbf{first die}};
\node at (-4.5,0.5) {\textbf{second die}};
\node at (-2.5,3.5) {\textbf{+}};
\node at (-1.5,3.5) {\textbf{1}};
\node at (-0.5,3.5) {\textbf{2}};
\node at (0.5,3.5) {\textbf{3}};
\node at (1.5,3.5) {\textbf{4}};
\node at (2.5,3.5) {\textbf{5}};
\node at (3.5,3.5) {\textbf{6}};
\node at (-2.5,2.5) {\textbf{1}};
\node at (-2.5,1.5) {\textbf{2}};
\node at (-2.5,0.5) {\textbf{3}};
\node at (-2.5,-0.5) {\textbf{4}};
\node at (-2.5,-1.5) {\textbf{5}};
\node at (-2.5,-2.5) {\textbf{6}};
\node at (-1.5,2.5) {2};
\node at (-0.5,2.5) {3};
\node at (0.5,2.5) {4};
\node at (1.5,2.5) {5};
\node at (2.5,2.5) {6};
\node at (3.5,2.5) {7};
\node at (-1.5,1.5) {3};
\node at (-1.5,0.5) {4};
\node at (-1.5,-0.5) {5};
\node at (-1.5,-1.5) {6};
\node at (-1.5,-2.5) {7};
\node at (-0.5,1.5) {4};
\node at (0.5,1.5) {5};
\node at (1.5,1.5) {6};
\node at (2.5,1.5) {7};
\node at (3.5,1.5) {8};
\node at (-0.5,0.5) {5};
\node at (-0.5,-0.5) {6};
\node at (-0.5,-1.5) {7};
\node at (-0.5,-2.5) {8};
\node at (0.5,0.5) {6};
\node at (0.5,-0.5) {7};
\node at (0.5,-1.5) {8};
\node at (0.5,-2.5) {9};
\draw (0.5,-2.5) circle (0.4);
\node at (1.5,0.5) {7};
\node at (2.5,0.5) {8};
\node at (3.5,0.5) {9};
\draw (3.5,0.5) circle (0.4);
\node at (1.5,-0.5) {8};
\node at (2.5,-0.5) {9};
\draw (2.5,-0.5) circle (0.4);
\node at (3.5,-0.5) {10};
\draw (3.5,-0.5) circle (0.4);
\node at (1.5,-1.5) {9};
\draw (1.5,-1.5) circle (0.4);
\node at (1.5,-2.5) {10};
\draw (1.5,-2.5) circle (0.4);
\node at (2.5,-1.5) {10};
\draw (2.5,-1.5) circle (0.4);
\node at (3.5,-1.5) {11};
\draw (3.5,-1.5) circle (0.4);
\node at (2.5,-2.5) {11};
\draw (2.5,-2.5) circle (0.4);
\node at (3.5,-2.5) {12};
\draw (3.5,-2.5) circle (0.4);
\node at (7,0.5) {$\text{P}(\text{sum}\geqslant9)=\dfrac{\text{10}}{\text{36}}=\dfrac{\text{5}}{\text{18}}$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{tcolorbox}

\pagebreak

[]{#ex22q}

#### [Exercise 2.2](#ex22a) {-}

For most of the following questions, it is recommended to write out a full list of all the possible equally likely outcomes. There may be times when a full list would be excessive in length, in which case careful multiplication can reveal the *number* of outcomes, and only the *relevant* ones may be needed to be listed.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item A coin is tossed and a cubical, fair die numbered 1 to 6 is rolled. Write down:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item A list of all outcomes.
        \item $\text{P}(\text{even and tails})$
    \end{enumerate}
    \item Each morning, a teacher picks at random whether to start the day with tea, coffee or apple juice, and also picks at random which one of muesli, granola, toast or porridge to eat for breakfast. By first listing all the possible ways the teacher may start their day, determine the probability that on any particular morning the teacher has:
        \begin{enumerate}
            \item Tea and toast.
            \item muesli but not coffee.
            \item Neither porridge nor tea.
        \end{enumerate}
    \item A coin is tossed three times. Find the probability that it shows:
    \begin{tikzpicture}[remember picture,overlay]
        \node[xshift=1cm,yshift=0.5cm,anchor=north west] {%
        \includegraphics[width=0.3\textwidth]{threecoins.png}};
        \end{tikzpicture}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Tails on two consecutive tosses.
        \item Tails at least twice.
    \end{enumerate}   
    \item A random number is chosen from 1 to 140, inclusive. By first listing all relevant results, determine the probability of the number chosen having digits that sum to 3.
    \item Two fair, cubical dice are rolled. Find the probability that the product of the numbers obtained is less than 5.
    \item A tetrahedral (four-sided) die numbered 1 to 4 and a cubical die numbered 1 to 6 are rolled. Find the probability that at least one of the numbers obtained is 2 or less.
    \item Five coins are placed in a bag, with values of 1p, 2p, 5p, 10p and 20p. Two coins are chosen at random, one after another, with the second chosen without replacement. Calculate:
    \begin{tikzpicture}[remember picture,overlay]
    \node[xshift=0cm,yshift=0cm,anchor=north west] {%
    \includegraphics[width=0.25\textwidth]{coins.png}};
    \end{tikzpicture}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $\text{P}(\text{the 20p is selected first})$
        \item $\text{P}(\text{the 20p is selected as one of the two coins})$
        \item $\text{P}(\text{total of the two coins}\geqslant\text{15p})$
        \item $\text{P}(\text{total of the two coins}<\text{12p})$
    \end{enumerate}
    \item Two friends, Alex and Charlie, are part of a class which contains eight pupils in total. One pupil from the class is randomly chosen to hand out mini-whiteboards, and a different pupil is chosen to hand out pens.
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Determine the number of possible ways the two tasks can be assigned to two pupils.
        \item Hence, find the probability that:
        \begin{enumerate}
            \item Alex is chosen to hand out the mini-whiteboards, and Charlie is chosen to hand out the pens.
            \item Neither Alex nor Charlie are chosen for either task.
            \item At least one of Alex and Charlie are chosen to perform a task.
        \end{enumerate}
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

## Tree Diagrams

Tree diagrams can be created to map out the possible outcomes of a random experiment, and they are especially useful when considering two or more events. For example, consider a game involving rolling a cubical die and picking a playing card at random from a standard set, with an interest in the chance of the die landing on a *six* and the card picked being a *diamond*:

\vspace{-0.3cm}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[grow=right,scale=1.1]
\tikzstyle{level 1}=[level distance=3.5cm, sibling distance=3.5cm]
\tikzstyle{level 2}=[level distance=3.5cm, sibling distance=1.75cm]
\tikzstyle{bag} = [text width=4em, text centered]
\tikzstyle{end} = [circle, minimum width=3pt,fill, inner sep=0pt]
\node[bag] {}
    child {
        node[bag] {not six}        
            child {
                node[end, label=right:
                    {diamond}] {}
                edge from parent
                node[above] {}
                node[below]  {$\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{4}}$}
            }
            child {
                node[end, label=right:
                    {not diamond}] {}
                edge from parent
                node[above] {$\dfrac{\text{3}}{\text{4}}$}
                node[below]  {}
            }
            edge from parent 
            node[above] {}
            node[below]  {$\dfrac{\text{5}}{\text{6}}$}
    }
    child {
        node[bag] {six}        
        child {
                node[end, label=right:
                    {diamond\hspace{1.4cm}$\text{P}(\text{six }\textbf{and}\text{  diamond})=\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{6}}\times\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{4}}=\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{24}}$}] {}
                edge from parent
                node[above] {}
                node[below]  {$\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{4}}$}
            }
            child {
                node[end, label=right:
                    {not diamond}] {}
                edge from parent
                node[above] {$\dfrac{\text{3}}{\text{4}}$}
                node[below]  {}
            }
        edge from parent         
            node[above] {$\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{6}}$}
            node[below]  {}
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{figure}

Tree diagrams should be complete with each *branch* labelled with a *probability*, and each *node* labelled with an *event*, as above. Since each split into *sibling branches* should show all the possible things that can happen next, the probabilities on sibling branches should add to 1.

\[\text{i.e.}\;\frac{\text{1}}{\text{6}}+\frac{\text{5}}{\text{6}}=\text{1}\;\text{ and }\;\frac{\text{3}}{\text{4}}+\frac{\text{1}}{\text{4}}=\text{1}\]

Multiplying the probabilities along a path of branches gives the probability of all those events occurring together.

Note that in the tree diagram above the probability of the card being a diamond does not change depending on whether the roll of a die gives a six or not; this is because the roll of a die and the drawing of a card are **independent**. In the following example the probabilities on the second stage of the tree diagram do change depending on the result of the first stage, since the first and second draws of a cube are **not independent**.

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!,colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} From a bag containing four blue and six red cubes one is drawn at random, followed by another without replacing the first. Find the probability that two red cubes are drawn.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution: }

\vspace{-0.3cm}

\begin{tikzpicture}[grow=right]
\tikzstyle{level 1}=[level distance=3.5cm, sibling distance=3.5cm]
\tikzstyle{level 2}=[level distance=3.5cm, sibling distance=1.75cm]
\tikzstyle{bag} = [text width=4em, text centered]
\tikzstyle{end} = [circle, minimum width=3pt,fill, inner sep=0pt]
\node[bag] {}
    child {
        node[bag] {red}        
            child {
                node[end, label=right:
                    {red\hspace{1.2cm}$\text{P}(\text{both red})=\dfrac{\text{6}}{\text{10}}\times\dfrac{\text{5}}{\text{9}}=\dfrac{\text{30}}{\text{90}}=\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{3}}$}] {}
                edge from parent
                node[above] {}
                node[below]  {$\dfrac{\text{5}}{\text{9}}$}
            }
            child {
                node[end, label=right:
                    {blue}] {}
                edge from parent
                node[above] {$\dfrac{\text{4}}{\text{9}}$}
                node[below]  {}
            }
            edge from parent 
            node[above] {}
            node[below]  {$\dfrac{\text{6}}{\text{10}}$}
    }
    child {
        node[bag] {blue}        
        child {
                node[end, label=right:
                    {red}] {}
                edge from parent
                node[above] {}
                node[below]  {$\dfrac{\text{6}}{\text{9}}$}
            }
            child {
                node[end, label=right:
                    {blue}] {}
                edge from parent
                node[above] {$\dfrac{\text{3}}{\text{9}}$}
                node[below]  {}
            }
        edge from parent         
            node[above] {$\dfrac{\text{4}}{\text{10}}$}
            node[below]  {}
    };
\end{tikzpicture}

\end{tcolorbox}

\pagebreak

[]{#ex23q}

#### [Exercise 2.3](#ex23a) {-}

*Construct a tree diagram for each question*.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item A counter is selected at random from a bag containing four green counters and five yellow counters, and then a second counter is selected from the bag without replacing the first counter that was taken.
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Construct a tree diagram to represent the possible results.
        \item Find the probability that both counters selected are green.
        \item Find the probability that at least one of the counters selected is green.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item A game involves a player randomly selecting one card then a second card from a set of six blue cards and four red cards, with replacement. Calculate the probability of a player selecting two cards of the same colour.
    \item In Scotland, 41\% of the population have the blood type \textit{O positive (O+)}. A doctor in Scotland randomly selects two of their patients and asks for them to take part in a blood screening trial, as part of which their blood type will be checked. Calculate the probability that neither of the two patients has blood type \textit{O+}.
    \item A component used in wind turbines is tested at two points in the production process, and marked as either \textit{sufficient quality} or \textit{insufficient quality} on the first testing point and then \textit{pass} or \textit{fail} at the second testing point. A component that is not of sufficient quality at the first testing point will continue to be developed and may still pass at the second testing point. Three-quarters of the components are marked as \textit{sufficient quality} initially, of which nine-tenths go on to pass. Of those initially marked as \textit{insufficient quality}, only three-fifths go on to pass.
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Construct a tree diagram to represent this information.
        \item Determine the probability that a component selected at random passes at the second testing point.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item A bus for a particular route that is scheduled to run once per day is cancelled on 3\% of days. On the days when it is running, it is running late 12\% of the time.
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Construct a tree diagram to represent this information.
        \item Calculate the probability that the bus is running on time on any given day (i.e. not cancelled and not late).
    \end{enumerate}
    \item A pencil case contains pencils of three different types: four HB pencils, two 2B pencils and one 2H pencil. Two pencils are selected at random, without replacement.
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Construct a tree diagram to represent the types of the two pencils selected.
        \item Find the probability that both pencils selected are of the same type.
        \item Determine the probability that the two pencils selected both are of different types.
        \item Find the probability that neither pencil selected is an HB pencil.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item A motorist passes through three sets of traffic lights on their way to work each day. If the probability of having to stop at a red light for each set of lights is 0.6, 0.2 and 0.3 respectively then find the probability that:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item The motorist does not have to stop.
        \item The motorist has to stop at least once.
        \item The motorist has to stop at exactly two of the sets of lights.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}
\vspace{0.8cm}

\pagebreak

## Venn Diagrams and Set Notation

Venn diagrams are used to show which elements of a set belong to one or more subsets. They can offer an alternative way of representing information about a sample space that might otherwise be shown in a table. For instance, the *contingency table* below shows the twelve dishes from a restaurant's main course menu, broken down by whether or not each is *gluten-free* ($G$) and whether or not each is *vegan* ($V$). Beside it is a Venn diagram showing the same information.

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
\captionsetup[figure]{labelformat=empty}%
\begin{minipage}{.5\textwidth}
  \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{| c|c c|} 
      \hline
          & vegan & not vegan\\ 
      \hline
         gluten-free & 2 & 1\\ 
         not gluten-free & 3 & 6\\ 
     \hline
    \end{tabular}
\captionof{figure}{\textit{Contingency table}}
  \label{fig:testTable}
  \end{center}
  Where a table is used to show categorical data in terms of frequencies in statistics, it is often referred to as a \textbf{contingency table}.  
\end{minipage}%
  \centering
\begin{minipage}{.5\textwidth}
  \centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[fill=white, line width=0.5pt]
\draw (-0.3,-0.2) circle (1.2) (-0.3,0.9)  node [text=black,above] {$G$};
\draw (1.3,-0.2) circle (1.2) (1.3,0.9)  node [text=black,above] {$V$};
\draw (-2.5,-1.8) rectangle (3.5,1.8) node [text=black,above] {$S$};
\draw[fill=black] (-0.6,-0.1) circle (0.07);
\draw[fill=black] (0.5,0.1) circle (0.07);
\draw[fill=black] (0.6,-0.4) circle (0.07);
\draw[fill=black] (1.3,0) circle (0.07);
\draw[fill=black] (1.7,0.3) circle (0.07);
\draw[fill=black] (1.9,-0.4) circle (0.07);
\draw[fill=black] (-2,-0.7) circle (0.07);
\draw[fill=black] (-1,1.3) circle (0.07);
\draw[fill=black] (0.5,1.4) circle (0.07);
\draw[fill=black] (2,1.5) circle (0.07);
\draw[fill=black] (2.8,0.4) circle (0.07);
\draw[fill=black] (3,-1) circle (0.07);
\end{tikzpicture}
\captionof{figure}{\textit{Venn diagram}}
  \label{fig:testVenn}
\end{minipage}
\end{figure}

### Complements, Intersections and Unions {-}

So far, *descriptive* words have been used to refer to different sets of outcomes. Using *set notation*:

\begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
        \hline
            Description & Set notation & Name\\
        \hline \rule{0pt}{3ex}  
            \textit{not} $A$ & $\overline{A}$ or $A'$ & Complement of $A$\\
            $A$ \textit{and} $B$ & $A\cap B$ & Intersection of $A$ and $B$\\
            $A$ \textit{or} $B$ & $A\cup B$ & Union of $A$ and $B$\\[1ex]
        \hline
    \end{tabular}
\end{center}

Venn diagrams help illustrate these. Note that *capital letters* are commonly used to represent events.

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[t]{.33\textwidth}
  \centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[line width=0.5pt]
\fill [gray!15] (-2.1,-1.7) rectangle (3.1,1.5);
\fill [white] (-0.3,-0.2) circle (1.2);
\draw (-0.3,-0.2) circle (1.2) (-0.3,0.9)  node [text=black,above] {$A$};
\draw (1.3,-0.2) circle (1.2) (1.3,0.9)  node [text=black,above] {$B$};
\draw (-2.1,-1.7) rectangle (3.1,1.5) node [text=black,above] {$S$};
\draw[fill=gray!15,rounded corners] (-0.4,-2.8) rectangle (1.4,-2.1);
\node at (0.5,-2.41) {$\overline{A}$ or $A'$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{minipage}%
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[t]{.33\textwidth}
  \centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[fill=white, line width=0.5pt]
\scope
\clip (-0.3,-0.2) circle (1.2);
\fill[gray!15] (1.3,-0.2) circle (1.2);
\endscope
\draw (-0.3,-0.2) circle (1.2) (-0.3,0.9)  node [text=black,above] {$A$};
\draw (1.3,-0.2) circle (1.2) (1.3,0.9)  node [text=black,above] {$B$}
      (-2.1,-1.7) rectangle (3.1,1.5) node [text=black,above] {$S$};
\draw[fill=gray!15,rounded corners] (-0.4,-2.8) rectangle (1.4,-2.1);
\node at (0.5,-2.41) {$A\cap B$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{minipage}%
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[t]{.33\textwidth}
  \centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[fill=white, line width=0.5pt]
\fill[gray!15] (-0.3,-0.2) circle (1.2) (-0.3,0.9);
\draw[fill=gray!15] (1.3,-0.2) circle (1.2) (1.3,0.9)  node [text=black,above] {$B$};
\draw (-0.3,-0.2) circle (1.2) (-0.3,0.9)  node [text=black,above] {$A$};
\draw (-2.1,-1.7) rectangle (3.1,1.5) node [text=black,above] {$S$};
\draw[fill=gray!15,rounded corners] (-0.4,-2.8) rectangle (1.4,-2.1);
\node at (0.5,-2.41) {$A\cup B$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{minipage}
\end{figure}

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!,colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} A dish is picked at random from the twelve on the main course menu shown in the Venn diagram above. State the value of $\text{P}(G\cap V)$.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:}

$\text{P}(G\cap V)=\dfrac{\text{2}}{\text{12}}=\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{6}}$

\end{tcolorbox}

\pagebreak

[]{#ex24q}

#### [Exercise 2.4](#ex24a) {-}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item One of the nine elements shown on the Venn diagram below is selected at random. State:
    \begin{multicols}{2}
    \null\vfill
    \vspace{-0.4cm}
        \begin{enumerate}
        \begin{multicols}{2}
            \item $\text{P}(A)$
            \item $\text{P}(B)$
            \item $\text{P}(\overline{A})$
            \item $\text{P}(\overline{B})$
            \item $\text{P}(A\cap B)$
            \item $\text{P}(A\cup B)$
            \item $\text{P}(\overline{A}\cap B)$
            \item $\text{P}(A\cap \overline{B})$
        \end{multicols}
        \end{enumerate}  
    \vfill\null
    \columnbreak
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[fill=white, line width=0.5pt]
        \draw (-0.7,0) circle (1.2) (-0.7,1.2)  node [text=black,above] {\text{A}};
        \draw (0.7,0) circle (1.2) (0.7,1.2)  node [text=black,above] {\text{B}}
              (-2.8,-1.5) rectangle (2.8,1.7) node [text=black,below right] {$S$};
        \draw[fill=black] (-2.2,0.4) circle (0.08);
        \draw[fill=black] (-1.9,-0.8) circle (0.08);
        \draw[fill=black] (0,0.4) circle (0.08);
        \draw[fill=black] (-0.9,-0.6) circle (0.08);
        \draw[fill=black] (-0.8,0.1) circle (0.08);
        \draw[fill=black] (0.9,-0.2) circle (0.08);
        \draw[fill=black] (0.2,0.1) circle (0.08);
        \draw[fill=black] (2.1,0.2) circle (0.08);
        \draw[fill=black] (0.1,-0.5) circle (0.08);
    \end{tikzpicture}
    \end{center}
    \end{multicols}
    \vspace{-0.4cm}
    \item The Venn diagram below, containing events $M$ and $N$, shows the \textit{probabilities} associated with each subset.
    \begin{multicols}{2}
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[fill=white, line width=0.5pt]
    \draw (-0.7,0) circle (1.2) (-0.7,1.2)  node [text=black,above] {$M$};
    \draw (0.7,0) circle (1.2) (0.7,1.2)  node [text=black,above] {$N$}
      (-2.5,-1.7) rectangle (2.5,1.7) node [text=black,below right] {$S$};
    \node at (-2,-1.3) {0.3};
    \node at (-1,0.5) {0.15};
    \node at (1.1,-0.2) {0.2};
    \node at (0,0) {$x$};
    \end{tikzpicture}
    \end{center}
    \columnbreak
    \null
    \vspace{-0.3cm}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item State the value of $x$.
        \item State:
        \begin{enumerate}
            \item \text{P}($N$).
            \item \text{P}($\overline{M}$).
            \item \text{P}($M\cup N$).
        \end{enumerate}
    \end{enumerate}
    \vfill\null
    \end{multicols}
    \vspace{-0.5cm}
    \item Copy the Venn diagram below, writing each of the integers from 1 to 16 inclusive in an appropriate position. Then, given that an integer from 1 to 16 inclusive is selected at random, state the value of:
    \begin{multicols}{2}
    \begin{center}
        \begin{tikzpicture}[fill=white, line width=0.5pt]
        \draw (-0.9,0) circle (1.2) (-0.9,1.5)  node [text=black] {\text{prime}};
        \draw (0.9,0) circle (1.2) (0.9,1.5)  node [text=black] {\text{odd}}
        (-3.2,-1.6) rectangle (3.2,1.8) node [text=black,below right] {$S$};
    \end{tikzpicture}
    \end{center}
    \columnbreak
    \null\vfill
    \vspace{-0.2cm}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \begin{multicols}{2}
            \item \text{P}(prime)
            \item \text{P}(odd)
            \item \text{P}( $\overline{\text{prime}}$ )
            \item \text{P}( $\overline{\text{odd}}$ )
            \item \text{P}(prime $\cap$ odd)
            \item \text{P}(prime $\cup$ odd)
            \item \text{P}($\overline{\text{prime}}$ $\cap$ odd)
            \item \text{P}(prime $\cap$ $\overline{\text{odd}}$)
        \end{multicols}
    \end{enumerate}
    \vfill\null
    \end{multicols}
    \vspace{-0.5cm}
    \item 100 pupils are asked whether or not they love cats, and whether or not they love dogs, with the results shown in the table below. One pupil is to be selected at random. Let event $C$ represent "a pupil who loves cats is selected", and event $D$ represent "a pupil who loves dogs is selected". By first representing this information on a Venn diagram, find: 
    \begin{multicols}{2}
    \begin{enumerate}
    \begin{multicols}{2}
        \item \text{P}($C\cap D$)
        \item \text{P}($C\cup D$)
        \item \text{P}($\overline{C}\cap\overline{D}$)
        \item \text{P}($\overline{C}\cup\overline{D}$)
        \item \text{P}($C\cap\overline{D}$)
        \item \text{P}($\overline{C}\cap D$)
        \item \text{P}($C\cup\overline{D}$)
        \item \text{P}($\overline{C}\cup D$)
    \end{multicols}
    \end{enumerate}
    \columnbreak
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{ c|c c }
           & love cats & do not love cats\\ 
      \hline
         love dogs & 34 & 12\\ 
         do not love dogs & 45 & 9
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    \vfill\null
    \end{multicols}
    \vspace{-0.2cm}
    \item Amongst a class of 24 pupils, seven are studying biology \textit{but not chemistry}, five are studying chemistry \textit{but not biology}, and six are studying neither biology nor chemistry. Find the probability that a pupil chosen at random is studying biology.
    \item Of the twenty air pollution monitoring stations in an urban area, nine are showing high levels of particulate matter (PM) in the air and eight are showing high levels of nitrogen dioxide (NO$_2$) in the air, whilst five are not showing high levels of either pollutant. Determine the probability that one of the twenty stations chosen at random is showing high levels of PM \textit{and} NO$_2$.
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

## The Addition Rule for the Union

If two sets $A$ and $B$ are such that *no outcomes are common to both* $A$ *and* $B$, then they are said to be **mutually exclusive**. This is sometimes visualised on a diagram by two *disjoint* circles, that do not intersect. In terms of probability theory, this means that the probability of the event `$A$ *and* $B$' is 0 (impossible).

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.5\textwidth}
  \centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[line width=0.5pt]
\draw (-0.8,-0.2) circle (1.2) (-0.8,0.9)  node [text=black,above] {$A$};
\draw (1.8,-0.2) circle (1.2) (1.8,0.9)  node [text=black,above] {$B$};
\draw (-2.6,-1.7) rectangle (3.6,1.5) node [text=black,below right] {$S$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{minipage}%
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.5\textwidth}
  \centering
\begin{tcolorbox}[title=For Mutually Exclusive events {$A$} and {$B$}:,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black, halign = center,halign title = center,width=8cm,height=1.8cm]
$\text{P}(A\cap B)=0$
\end{tcolorbox}
\end{minipage}
\end{figure}

If two sets $A$ and $B$ are such that *every outcome in the sample space lies in at least one of* $A$ *or* $B$, then they are said to be **exhaustive**. This can be visualised on a Venn diagram by crossing out the area of the sample space outside of the two circles. This means that the probability of the event `$A$ *or* $B$' is 1 (certain).

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.5\textwidth}
  \centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[line width=0.5pt]
\fill[pattern=north east lines] (-2.6,-1.7) rectangle (3.6,1.5);
\fill[white] (-0.4,-0.2) circle (1.2);
\fill[white] (1.4,-0.2) circle (1.2);
\draw (-0.4,-0.2) circle (1.2) (-0.4,0.9)  node [text=black,above] {$A$};
\draw (1.4,-0.2) circle (1.2) (1.4,0.9)  node [text=black,above] {$B$};
\draw (-2.6,-1.7) rectangle (3.6,1.5) node [text=black,below right] {$S$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{minipage}%
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.5\textwidth}
  \centering
\begin{tcolorbox}[title=For Exhaustive events {$A$} and {$B$}:,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black, halign = center,halign title = center,width=8cm,height=1.8cm]
$\text{P}(A\cup B)=1$
\end{tcolorbox}
\end{minipage}
\end{figure}

In addition to the special cases above (when events are mutually exclusive or exhaustive), the following rules apply to *all* events $A,B$:

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.4\textwidth}
  \centering
\begin{tcolorbox}[title=The Complement of {$A$}:,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black, halign = center,halign title = center,width=6cm,height=1.8cm]
$\text{P}(A)+\text{P}(\overline{A})=\text{1}$
\end{tcolorbox}
\end{minipage}%
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.6\textwidth}
  \centering
\begin{tcolorbox}[title=The Addition Rule for events $A$ and $B$:, halign title = center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black, halign = center,width=9cm,height=1.8cm]
$\text{P}(A \cup B)=\text{P}(A)+\text{P}(B)-\text{P}(A \cap B)$
\end{tcolorbox}
\end{minipage}
\end{figure}

It should be noted that there are generally numerous different ways to approach probability problems, including using a Venn diagram, a tree diagram or formulae. Whilst the problems on the next page have been designed to allow practice using formulae, a flexible approach may help.

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!,colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} Given events $F$ and $G$ such that ${\text{P}(\overline{F})=\text{0.3}}$, ${\text{P}(G)=\text{0.4}}$ and ${\text{P}(F\cap G)=\text{0.1}}$ show that $F$ and $G$ are exhaustive.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:} \begin{align*}
\text{P}(F\cup G) & = \text{P}(F)+\text{P}(G)-\text{P}(F \cap G)\vspace{-0.5cm}\\
& = \text{0.7}+\text{0.4}-\text{0.1}\\
& = \text{1}\hspace{0.5cm}\therefore\text{ events $F$ and $G$ are exhaustive}
\end{align*}
\end{tcolorbox}

\pagebreak

[]{#ex25q}

#### [Exercise 2.5](#ex25a) {-}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Given events $X$ and $Y$ such that $\text{P}(X\cap Y)=\text{0.3}$, $\text{P}(X)=\text{0.4}$ and $\text{P}(Y)=\text{0.5}$, find $\text{P}(X\cup Y)$.
    \item If $\text{P}(\overline{A})=\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{5}}$, $\text{P}(B)=\dfrac{\text{2}}{\text{3}}$ and $\text{P}(A\cap B)=\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{2}}$, determine the value of $\text{P}(A\cup B)$.
    \item Calculate the value of $\text{P}(E\cup F)$ given $\text{P}(E)=\text{0.32}$, $\text{P}(F)=\text{0.29}$ and that events $E$ and $F$ are \textit{mutually exclusive}.
    \item Given mutually exclusive events $C$ and $D$ such that $\text{P}(\overline{C})=\dfrac{\text{5}}{\text{7}}$ and $\text{P}(D)=\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{2}}$, find $\text{P}(C\cup D)$.
    \item Calculate the value of $\text{P}(A\cap B)$ if $\text{P}(A)=\text{0.591}$, $\text{P}(B)=\text{0.472}$ and $\text{P}(A\cup B)=\text{0.9}$.
    \item If $\text{P}(\overline{X})=\dfrac{\text{17}}{\text{80}}$, $\text{P}(\overline{Y})=\dfrac{\text{29}}{\text{40}}$ and $\text{P}(X\cup Y)=\dfrac{\text{19}}{\text{20}}$, determine $\text{P}(X\cap Y)$.
    \item Given \textit{exhaustive} events $V$ and $W$ such that $\text{P}(V)=\text{0.4}$ and $\text{P}(\overline{W})=\text{0.2}$, find $\text{P}(V\cap W)$.
    \item Given events $H$ and $J$ such that $\text{P}(H)=\dfrac{\text{3}}{\text{8}}$, \mbox{\text{$\text{P}(H\cup J)=\dfrac{\text{7}}{\text{9}}$}} and $\text{P}(H\cap J)=\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{4}}$, find $\text{P}(\overline{J})$.
    \item If $X$ and $Y$ are mutually exclusive, $\text{P}(X)=\text{0.3}$ and $\text{P}(\overline{Y})=\text{0.4}$, calculate $\text{P}(X\cup Y)$.
    \item Events $A$ and $B$ are exhaustive. If $\text{P}(A)=\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{3}}$ and $\text{P}(B\cap A)=\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{6}}$, calculate $\text{P}(\overline{B})$.
    \item In a fish and chip shop, the probability that a customer's order includes both fish and chips is 0.7, whilst the probability that an order will include at least one of fish or chips is 0.95. If the probability that an order contains chips is 0.8, calculate the probability that an order contains fish.
    \item If, for exhaustive events $M$ and $N$, $\text{P}(M\cap N)=\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{4}}$ and $\text{P}(M)=\text{P}(N)$, calculate $\text{P}(\overline{N})$.
    \item Given events $R$ and $Q$ such that $\text{P}(\overline{Q})=\text{0.11}$, $\text{P}(R\cap Q)=\text{0.75}$ and $\text{P}(R\cup Q)=\text{0.95}$, find $\text{P}(\overline{R})$.
    \item For each pair of events, determine whether they are exhaustive or not:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $X$ and $Y$ given $\text{P}(X)=\text{0.4}$, $\text{P}(Y)=\text{0.7}$ and $\text{P}(X \cap Y)=\text{0.2}$.
        \item $R$ and $T$ given $\text{P}(R)=\dfrac{\text{4}}{\text{5}}$, $\text{P}(\overline{T})=\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{10}}$ and $\text{P}(T \cap R)=\dfrac{\text{7}}{\text{10}}$.
        \item $E$ and $F$ given $\text{P}(E)=\text{0.31}$, $\text{P}(F)=\text{0.82}$ and $\text{P}(E \cap F)=\text{0.28}$.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item For each pair of events, determine whether they are mutually exclusive or not:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $W$ and $V$ given $\text{P}(W)=\text{0.35}$, $\text{P}(V)=\text{0.45}$ and $\text{P}(W \cup V)=\text{0.5}$.
        \item $X$ and $Y$ given $\text{P}(X)=\dfrac{\text{1}}{\text{3}}$, $\text{P}(\overline{Y})=\dfrac{\text{4}}{\text{5}}$ and $\text{P}(X \cup Y)=\dfrac{\text{8}}{\text{15}}$.
        \item $A$ and $B$ given $\text{P}(\overline{A})=\text{0.5}$, $\text{P}(\overline{B})=\text{0.8}$ and $\text{P}(\overline{B} \cap \overline{A})=\text{0.3}$.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

[]{#re2q}

## [Review Exercise](#re2a) {-}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item A box contains five red balls numbered from 1 to 5, and four yellow balls numbered from 1 to 4. A ball is selected at random from the box. Let \textit{yellow} be the event "the ball selected is yellow".
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item State \text{P}($\overline{\text{yellow}}$ $\cup$ 1).
    \end{enumerate}
    The ball previously selected is returned to the box. Two balls are then randomly taken from the box, without replacement.
    \begin{enumerate}[resume]
        \item Determine the probability that neither of the two balls taken from the box are yellow.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item The table below shows the pupil numbers within a school corridor during a particular period, broken down by subject and course level.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{| c|c c|}
        \hline
             & National 5 & Higher\\
        \hline
            English & 21 & 15\\
            History & 17 & 11\\
        \hline
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    A pupil is selected at random to run an errand. State:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item \text{P}(\text{English} $\cap$ \text{National 5})
        \item \text{P}(\text{History} $\cup$ \text{Higher})
    \end{enumerate}
    \item For the exhaustive events $E$ and $F$, such that $\text{P}(F\cap E)=\text{0.7}$ and $\text{P}(\overline{E})=\text{0.2}$, determine $\text{P}(F)$.
    \item A parking ticket machine in a remote location in the Cairngorms National Park takes payment by contactless card reader, which connects to the banking network via a 3G phone signal. On some days, the connection fails and the machine cannot take payment. When the humidity is high, the connection will fail on 18\% of days. When the humidity is not high, the connection will fail on 7\% of days. Humidity is high in the area on 34\% of days. Determine the probability on a randomly selected day of the year that the parking machine can take payment.
    \item A board game begins with an \textit{defending} player rolling a tetrahedral die with sides numbered from 1 to 4, and a \textit{attacking} player rolling a cubical die with sides numbered from 1 to 6. The player who obtains the highest number earns a point, with a tie leading to the dice being rolled again.
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Find the probability that the first point is earned, by either player, without the dice having to be rolled again following a tie.
        \item Find the probability that the attacking player earns the first point.
    \end{enumerate}
    If the attacking player wins three points in a row, they are then awarded a \textit{bonus roll} in which they roll \textit{both} dice and gain a bonus point if the product of the numbers obtained is greater than 16.
    \begin{enumerate}[resume]
        \item Determine the probability that a bonus roll results in a bonus point.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item The 100 members of an orchestra are shown in the table below, broken down by the section they are in and whether they can attend the upcoming concert.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{| c|c c c c|}
        \hline
             & String & Woodwind & Brass & Percussion\\
        \hline
            attend & 56 & 12 & 10 & 4\\
            not attend & 8 & 4 & 5 & 1\\
        \hline
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    A member of the orchestra is selected at random. Let $B$ be the event the member selected is in the \textit{brass} section and $A$ that they can \textit{attend} the upcoming concert. State $\text{P}(B\cup \overline{A})$.
    \item Exhaustive events $A$, $B$ and $C$ are such that $\text{P}(A)=\text{0.25}$, $\text{P}(A\cap B)=\text{0.1}$, $\text{P}(\overline{B})=\text{0.3}$ and $\text{P}(A\cup C)=\text{0.6}$. Events $A$ and $C$ are mutually exclusive. Determine the value of $\text{P}(B\cap C)$.
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak
